strengths: The core prototype-based coupling and one-step generator concept remains creative and potentially impactful by eliminating ODE solvers at inference.

The authors corrected the derivation error in the discretized importance weights (restoring the squared denominator) and provided a principled SNIS connection from the continuous velocity to the discrete estimator, along with clear justification of σ-regularized invertibility and reverse-time integration.

They propose substantial additions: quantitative metrics on MNIST/CIFAR-10, comparisons with Rectified Flow, OT-CFM, and one-/few-step distillation methods, detailed wall-clock analyses, and comprehensive ablations (σ, buffer N/K, ODE tolerance).

The planned variance analysis for SNIS in higher dimensions (including ESS tracking, adaptive neighborhoods, rejection sampling, and K-NN/ANN proposals) and a theoretical error propagation bound would significantly strengthen methodological soundness.
Clarifications on notation and the removal of overstated claims (e.g., near-monotonicity) improve the conceptual presentation.
weaknesses: Despite the thorough plans, the current submission still lacks the quantitative evaluations, baseline comparisons, and scalability studies; practical effectiveness and robustness remain unsubstantiated at review time.

SNIS in high dimensions can suffer from weight degeneracy; while mitigations and monitoring are proposed, there is no present empirical or theoretical variance characterization in the paper to confirm feasibility across realistic settings.

Training requires reverse-time ODE solves for prototype computation; the manuscript does not yet quantify end-to-end wall-clock trade-offs, amortization strategies, or demonstrate that training overhead is offset by one-step inference.

Theoretical guarantees connecting approximate prototypes to the pushforward quality are only outlined for a revision; no formal statements or proofs are currently included, leaving the main claims dependent on future work.
reviewers.summary_comment: The rebuttal meaningfully improves methodological clarity: it fixes the importance-weight expression, grounds the softmax weights in self-normalized importance sampling, and precisely motivates σ for invertibility and reverse-time integration. The authors also outline a substantial and appropriate expansion of experiments (FID/IS, baselines, wall-clock profiling, CIFAR-10) and propose a thoughtful analysis of SNIS variance with concrete mitigations and monitoring, plus theoretical statements linking prototype and generator errors. These steps, if executed, would likely elevate the paper to acceptability. However, in its current form the submission still lacks the quantitative evidence, runtime analyses, and formal results necessary to substantiate the central claims and assess scalability. Given the strong novelty but incomplete evaluation, I recommend rejection at this time, with encouragement to resubmit once the promised results, analyses, and theoretical additions are incorporated.

strengths: Conceptually appealing one-step sampling strategy: compute per-target prototypes by integrating an explicit Flow Matching velocity backward (Eq. (2)) and train a direct generator from prototypes to targets, avoiding ODE solves at inference.

Method leverages an explicit FM velocity (Eq. (1)) and a practical SNIS-based discretization with a buffer; the rebuttal identifies and corrects a concrete mathematical error in the importance weights (missing square in the denominator) and outlines a clear derivation.

The authors provide improved methodological clarity in the rebuttal: separating the generator g_θ from the velocity v, formalizing σ>0 invertibility of the conditional map φ_{t,x1}(x0) = (1 − t + σ t) x0 + t x1, and clarifying reverse-time integration and stability near t≈1.

Planned additions are substantive: an ideal-case correctness proposition linking training pairs (X0, x1) to recovery of the σ-smoothed pushforward, a W2–MSE coupling bound, error-propagation analysis for SNIS and ODE integration, as well as expanded quantitative experiments with baselines (Rectified Flow, OT-CFM, InstaFlow), ablations (σ, buffer size N, solver tolerances), and wall-clock/runtime analyses.
weaknesses: As submitted, theoretical guarantees remain absent: there is no proof that prototype-based training recovers the σ-smoothed pushforward, nor bounds connecting SNIS bias/variance and ODE numerical error to generator risk; the need for σ>0 is discussed heuristically. The importance-weighting expression in Eq. (1)–(SNIS discretization) contains a mathematical error in the current draft, undermining soundness.

Empirical evaluation is inadequate: results are largely qualitative (toy 2D, MNIST, color transfer) without quantitative metrics (e.g., FID/IS, precision/recall, MMD/Wasserstein) or comparisons to relevant baselines (Rectified Flow, OT-CFM, one-/few-step distillation), so effectiveness and scalability cannot be assessed.

Computational trade-offs are unsubstantiated: the cost of prototype computation (reverse-time ODE with buffer-sized velocity evaluations) is not analyzed, and there is no wall-clock comparison to standard FM/CFM or rectified flows, nor sensitivity to dimension, buffer size N, or solver tolerances.

Some claims remain unverified: the "almost monotonic" coupling is not quantified; stability and behavior near t≈1 are not empirically analyzed; and performance on more challenging datasets beyond MNIST and simple color transfer is unspecified.
reviewers.summary_comment: The paper proposes an interesting one-step sampling approach for Flow Matching by pairing target samples with prototypes obtained via reverse-time integration of an explicit velocity field and then learning a direct generator from prototypes to targets. The rebuttal improves clarity (correcting the SNIS weighting, separating notation, and formalizing σ>0 invertibility) and outlines substantial theoretical and empirical additions, including an ideal-case correctness result, error bounds, and comprehensive quantitative evaluation with baselines and runtime analyses. However, these elements are not present in the current submission. As it stands, the paper still lacks rigorous theoretical guarantees, contains a nontrivial mathematical error in the discretization, and presents only qualitative experiments without baselines, making the core claims insufficiently supported. I encourage the authors to incorporate the promised theory, correct the mathematical issues, and add the proposed quantitative evaluations and analyses; with these in place, the work could become a solid contribution. In its current form, I recommend rejection.

strengths: Practically motivated idea: learn a one-step generator by training on prototype–target pairs constructed via an explicit flow-matching velocity, removing ODE solves at inference and potentially reducing variance and trajectory curvature.

Clear procedural description with Algorithm 1 and Algorithm 2, including labeled conditioning, buffer-based importance sampling, and the role of sigma-regularization to ensure invertibility and address low-dimensional manifold issues (illustrated in synthetic 2D examples).

The author response commits to substantial improvements: adding quantitative metrics (FID/IS/precision–recall, MMD/Wasserstein, LPIPS/ΔE), strong baselines (Rectified Flow, OT-CFM, InstaFlow/PAGODA), detailed wall-clock/memory profiling, extensive ablations (sigma, buffer N/K, solver tolerances, buffer composition), and code release.

Acknowledges and plans to correct a methodological error in the discretized importance weights used in the velocity estimator derived from Eq. (1), with a step-by-step SNIS derivation and consideration of rejection sampling to mitigate self-normalization bias.

Plans to quantify qualitative claims (e.g., near-monotone couplings and prototype sufficiency) with measurable statistics on trajectory straightness and intersection rates, and to analyze SNIS behavior via ESS and adaptive proposals, which would improve practical relevance.
weaknesses: In the current submission, evaluation remains limited and largely qualitative (toy 2D, MNIST, simple color transfer) without standard metrics or comparisons to strong baselines; there is no wall-clock or memory profiling to substantiate practical benefits.

Key methodological claims (near-monotone couplings, adequacy of approximate prototypes) lack theoretical guarantees and are not empirically validated at scale; the discretized weighting in the velocity estimator has an error that is only addressed in the rebuttal and not in the paper.

Prototype computation via reverse-time ODE with buffer-based SNIS is potentially expensive, and the paper does not quantify cost or scaling with dimension, buffer size N/K, or solver tolerances; practical deployment trade-offs are unclear.

Scalability to higher-dimensional image generation and sensitivity to buffer composition and SNIS bias are not demonstrated; latent-space and K-NN/ANN buffer variants are proposed in the rebuttal but absent from the current results.
reviewers.summary_comment: The paper presents a promising and practically motivated approach: using an explicit flow-matching velocity to construct prototype–target pairs and training a one-step generator to avoid ODE solves at inference. The algorithms are clearly described, and the sigma-regularization rationale is sensible. However, the current manuscript does not provide sufficient quantitative evidence or rigorous analysis to support practical significance. It lacks standard metrics and strong baselines, has no wall-clock/memory profiling, and contains a methodological error in the discretized importance weights for the velocity estimator derived from Eq. (1). Moreover, claims about trajectory monotonicity and prototype sufficiency are unsubstantiated, and scalability to higher-dimensional datasets is unclear. The rebuttal is thorough and proposes concrete fixes and comprehensive experiments (metrics, baselines, timing, ablations, corrected derivations, and code release) that would substantially strengthen the work. Acceptance should be based on demonstrated results rather than planned additions. I recommend rejection at this time and encourage resubmission after incorporating the promised experiments, analyses, and corrections.

strengths: The authors have materially improved the theoretical direction: replacing the overflow-based argument with a principled finite-state/Myhill–Nerode-style collapse under fixed precision and NoPE, and explicitly committing to formalize this with a finite-state transducer view.

Assumptions and regimes are now clearly separated: an inf-free regime for Section 5, precise characterization of NoPE subclasses (last-token plus finite multiset summaries), and APE-based constructions for m-cyclic languages; this resolves earlier contradictions.
They propose exact, finite-domain constructions (finite-code presence detection, bounded-count codes) that avoid dependence on ±inf and align with the finite-precision setting.

Concrete plans to strengthen rigor and presentation: explicit lemmas (finite-range, exchangeability, state summarization), full theorem statements, corrected attention calculations, and small mechanistic examples; plus sanity-check experiments to illustrate state collapse and attainable languages.
weaknesses: The core claims have been substantially revised (to finite-index collapse) and the corresponding rigorous proofs are not yet present in the manuscript; acceptance would hinge on seeing these full arguments and their technical details.

Key components remain at the proposal level: the formal definition of S_θ and G_θ, proofs of permutation invariance through residual/FFN stacks, and tight bounds on |S_θ| under fixed precision; without these, soundness is still below the bar.
No empirical or mechanistic evidence is available in the current submission; the promised sanity checks are valuable but not yet executed to support the practical implications.
Clarity issues persist in the submitted draft (notation, malformed equations/tables), and the significant reorganization needed to reflect the revised results is not yet realized.
reviewers.summary_comment: The second rebuttal is thoughtful and addresses the earlier concerns in a substantive way. Moving to a finite-state/Myhill–Nerode-style collapse under fixed precision and NoPE, clarifying regimes and assumptions, and proposing exact finite-code constructions are all strong steps that meaningfully improve the paper’s direction. However, these are major changes to the core claims that are not yet reflected by complete proofs, corrected constructions, or the clearer exposition required for acceptance. I encourage the authors to (i) fully formalize the finite-state transducer induced by the decoder by defining S_θ and G_θ and proving finite index via a right congruence; (ii) prove permutation invariance through attention, residuals, and FFNs under NoPE; (iii) provide explicit bounds on the number of representable states under fixed precision; (iv) complete the exact finite-code constructions for letter-set and bounded-count variants; and (v) include the proposed mechanistic sanity checks. With these elements in a cleaned-up manuscript, the work could be impactful in a future submission.

strengths: The rebuttal directly addresses the core soundness issues: it corrects the infinity-freeness assumption, removes overflow-based reasoning, and commits to aligning with numerically stable softmax and layer norm as total functions over fixed-precision domains.

The authors provide a concrete and more plausible technical direction via a finite-state (finite-index) collapse theorem under fixed precision and NoPE, including a precise restatement (Theorem 5.2′) and a set of supporting lemmas (finite-range, permutation invariance under NoPE with causal masking, deterministic update, and autoregressive augmentation).

They outline explicit finite-domain constructions for lower bounds (letter-set and bounded-count signatures under NoPE; m-cyclic languages with APE) that avoid appeals to universal approximation and ±inf, which, if fully developed, would substantially improve rigor.

They plan empirical sanity checks (state-collapse probing, recognition tasks) using standard, numerically stable implementations to connect theory to practice and to avoid reliance on pathological numerical behavior.
weaknesses: The current manuscript still contains the previously identified logical errors and unsound arguments; the corrected theorems, lemmas, and constructions are not yet present, and the central claim is materially revised, implying substantial rewrites beyond what can be assessed at review time.

Key proof obligations for the proposed finite-state framework remain unverified in the submission: formalizing the NoPE permutation invariance with causal masking, constructing and bounding the state space S_θ, defining the deterministic update G_θ, and handling the T-step autoregressive augmentation rigorously.
No evaluation is included in the paper as submitted; the proposed empirical probes are valuable but absent, leaving practical claims unsupported.
Significant clarity and presentation issues persist in the current text (malformed equations/tables, inconsistent notation), impeding a careful check of claims even before the proposed revisions.
reviewers.summary_comment: The rebuttal is constructive and substantially improves the conceptual direction: it replaces unsound overflow-based arguments with a finite-state perspective under fixed precision and NoPE, weakens the overstrong finite/co-finite claim, proposes explicit finite-domain constructions for lower bounds, and commits to numerically stable implementations and empirical sanity checks. These changes, if fully carried out, would address the main soundness concerns and better align the contribution with prior literature and practical implementations. However, the present manuscript still contains major logical errors, lacks the revised formal results and proofs, and provides no empirical evidence. Given the extent of the necessary changes and the absence of the promised developments in the submission, I must recommend rejection at this time. I encourage the authors to implement the outlined revisions, fully formalize the finite-state upper bound and constructions, correct the presentation, and include the proposed experiments for a stronger resubmission.

strengths: The authors fully retract the overflow-based argument and restate Assumption 5.1 as an explicit inf-free regime, committing to numerically stable softmax and layer normalization modeled as total maps on D_p^d, which aligns the theory with common implementations.

They propose a finite-state collapse proof for fixed-precision, O(1)-depth/width NoPE decoders and plan to quantify the induced state-space size |S_θ| as a function of precision and architectural constants, increasing technical rigor and practical interpretability.

They will replace hand-wavy lower bounds with explicit finite-domain constructions: per-letter presence and bounded-count summaries under NoPE, and precise m-cyclic constructions with APE, avoiding universal approximation and non-finite values.

They outline a dedicated positioning relative to prior literature (FOC[+; MOD], TC0, saturated/log-precision variants) to clarify novelty and scope, and plan targeted empirical sanity checks to corroborate the theoretical phenomena under standard, stable implementations.
weaknesses: As submitted, Sections 5–6 still rely on the unsound overflow reasoning, ambiguous assumption, and proof sketches; the promised changes constitute a substantive rewrite with altered main claims, which cannot be evaluated in the current manuscript.

The proposed finite-state collapse framework requires precise formalization that is not yet present: an explicit state-update function for one decoding step, a well-defined equivalence relation over prefixes, a finite-index/Myhill–Nerode-style proof, and proofs that residual connections, stable softmax, and layer norm preserve the finite-state property under fixed precision.

Significance hinges on careful comparison to existing bounds and on concrete quantification (e.g., bounds on |S_θ| and what subclasses are captured beyond letter-set languages); this positioning and quantification are not yet provided.

No empirical results are included; the planned experiments (state signature saturation, letter-set/m-cyclic tasks, ablations) are essential to support practical relevance but are absent in the current version, and clarity/notation issues (typos, malformed equations/tables) still impede assessment.
reviewers.summary_comment: The second rebuttal is constructive and addresses prior concerns head-on: it removes reliance on softmax overflow and non-finite values, adopts numerically stable softmax and layer norm, reframes the main result as a finite-state collapse under fixed precision and NoPE, commits to explicit finite-domain lower-bound constructions, and plans a clear comparison to prior bounds alongside targeted empirical checks. These planned revisions would materially improve soundness and practical relevance. However, the current submission still contains the original unsound arguments and sketchy proofs, and the revised technical core (finite-state collapse) is not yet formalized in the paper. The significance of the weakened upper bound will depend on rigorous statements, quantified constants, and positioning relative to FOC[+; MOD]/TC0. Absent the promised experiments and substantial clarity fixes, the work remains below the bar at this time. I encourage resubmission after incorporating the detailed formal proofs (including an explicit equivalence relation and finite-index argument), quantified bounds on state-space size, the comparative analysis, and the proposed empirical validations.

strengths: Clearly novel application of conditional flow matching to multi-sensor spatiotemporal fusion, unifying MODIS-to-Landsat downscaling and cloud/scanline gap filling via a mask-guided inpainting update (Alg. 3) within a single generative framework.

Compelling quantitative signal: CFM outperforms both diffusion and STARFM on held-out scenes (Table 2: SID 0.0186, SSIM 0.912, PSNR 31.8 vs diffusion 0.0271/0.891/30.0), achieves efficient inference with diminishing returns beyond 50 steps (Table 1: SSIM 0.912 at 50 vs 0.908 at 100), and shows strong gains from MODIS conditioning under heavy occlusion (Table 3, 75% cloud: SID 0.071 vs 0.167; SSIM 0.812 vs 0.723).

The rebuttal substantively strengthens evaluation breadth and realism: adding ESTARFM and FSDAF, a deep CNN spatiotemporal fusion model, and a transformer-style baseline; real-world cloud/shadow tests using near-date Landsat overlaps and small Sentinel-2 cross-sensor validation; and robustness analyses to QA mask errors and MODIS interpolation gaps.

Methodological soundness is reinforced with planned ablations on σ, alternative probability paths (rectified/VP-like), and inference stochasticity, plus an explicit correction to Eq. (4) and unified notation; uncertainty will be characterized via ensembles, coverage diagnostics, and CRPS.

Strong practical orientation: comprehensive CONUS dataset curation with metadata conditioning (DOY, sensor, MODIS flag), efficiency reporting (wall-clock and memory vs steps), and a commitment to code and preprocessing release improve reproducibility and applicability.
weaknesses: Many improvements (expanded baselines, real cloud/shadow evaluation, ablations, efficiency/uncertainty reporting, clarity fixes) are commitments not yet reflected in the manuscript; acceptance assumes these are delivered in revision.

Pipeline robustness under abrupt spectral changes (snow, fires) and QA mask misclassification remains a concern; even with a learned MODIS temporal imputer and robustness tests, performance may degrade without additional modalities.

Uncertainty quantification relies on Monte Carlo sampling and mild inference perturbations; calibration may remain limited without probabilistic training objectives, so reliability of intervals could be dataset-dependent.

Baseline breadth could still be constrained if the added transformer/CNN baselines are not trained at comparable scale or with matched conditioning; fairness and coverage across dynamic agricultural settings should be ensured.
reviewers.summary_comment: This work introduces SatFlow, a creative and forward-looking application of conditional flow matching to fuse MODIS and Landsat for high-resolution, gap-free reflectance generation and mask-guided inpainting (Alg. 3). The current results are strong (e.g., Table 2: SSIM 0.912, SID 0.0186, PSNR 31.8) and efficiency is compelling (Table 1), with clear benefits from MODIS under heavy occlusion (Table 3). The rebuttal convincingly addresses prior concerns with concrete additions: ESTARFM/FSDAF and deep/transformer baselines, real cloud/shadow evaluations, ablations on σ and probability paths, efficiency reporting, uncertainty diagnostics, clarity fixes (including correcting Eq. (4)), and code release. While several items are pending integration, the methodological core is sound, novelty and potential impact are high, and the planned revisions will substantially strengthen evaluation breadth and reproducibility. I recommend acceptance.

strengths: Promising application of conditional flow matching (CFM) for spatiotemporal fusion, with efficient linearized paths and few inference steps yielding strong reconstructions (e.g., Table 1: at 50 steps SSIM=0.912, SID=0.018, PSNR=31.8).

Clear empirical gains over two baselines on held-out years (Table 2: CFM vs. diffusion and STARFM with SSIM 0.912 vs. 0.891 and 0.852; SID 0.0186 vs. 0.0271 and 0.0481), and consistent improvements when MODIS is available in high cloud regimes (Table 3: at 75% cloud, SID 0.071 and SSIM 0.812 with MODIS vs. 0.167 and 0.723 without).

Comprehensive and concrete rebuttal plan addressing key concerns: addition of established baselines (ESTARFM/HISTARFM/STAIR/FSDAF and recent deep models), ablations (σ sensitivity, path choices, masking probabilities, modality contributions), real-cloud/shadow evaluation with QA-mask robustness, identical conditioning across methods, and computational efficiency reporting.

Large-scale CONUS dataset with held-out years (2012, 2015) suggests potential generalizability; the pipeline design integrating MODIS and Landsat composites is practically motivated for generating gap-free reflectance imagery.
weaknesses: Current evaluation remains narrow and potentially biased: only STARFM and a single diffusion variant are reported, with sparse configuration details; broader baselines and identical conditioning protocols promised in the rebuttal are not yet reflected in the results, leaving comparative claims insufficiently supported.

Cloud imputation is assessed only with synthetic masks; there is no quantitative evaluation on real clouds/shadows or QA-mask robustness in the submitted version, limiting confidence in operational applicability.

Methodological clarity/reproducibility issues persist in the manuscript: equation/notation errors (e.g., ambiguous integral in Eq. (4)), missing figure references (e.g., 'Figure ??'), and limited architectural/training details; code/data are not available to verify fairness and implementation.

Computational efficiency is not systematically reported beyond step counts; there are no wall-clock, memory, or throughput comparisons to diffusion and classical baselines to substantiate efficiency claims.

Potential conflation of downscaling with temporal propagation via the high-resolution composite remains a concern in the current results; modality ablations and strict pre-date-only composites are promised but not yet presented.
reviewers.summary_comment: SatFlow introduces a conditional flow matching framework to fuse MODIS and Landsat, generating high-resolution, gap-free reflectance imagery. The approach is technically plausible and initial results are encouraging, with clear improvements over a diffusion baseline and STARFM (e.g., SSIM=0.912, SID≈0.019 at 50 steps). The authors’ rebuttal is thorough and responsive, committing to add established fusion baselines, real-world cloud/shadow evaluations with robustness analyses, comprehensive ablations (σ, path choices, masking probabilities, modality contributions), identical conditioning across methods, and systematic efficiency reporting. However, these additions are not present in the current submission. As submitted, the evaluation breadth and fairness are inadequate to support the strength of the claims, real-cloud validation is missing, and clarity/reproducibility issues remain (equation/figure problems, limited implementation detail, no code). I encourage the authors to incorporate the promised experiments and fixes; with those in place, the work could be competitive in a future round, but at present it falls short of the rigor required.

strengths: Addresses a consequential, real-world problem: producing frequent, high-resolution, gap-free surface reflectance by fusing MODIS temporal context with Landsat spatial detail, directly relevant to agricultural and environmental monitoring.

Employs Conditional Flow Matching with a mask-respecting inpainting strategy; reported results are encouraging and efficient (e.g., Table 1 shows SSIM rising to 0.912 at 50 steps with diminishing returns thereafter).

Outperforms STARFM and a diffusion baseline on held-out scenes (Table 2: CFM SID 0.0186, SSIM 0.912, PSNR 31.8), and demonstrates that MODIS conditioning improves robustness as cloud cover increases (Table 3: at 75% clouds, SSIM 0.812 with MODIS vs. 0.723 without).
Constructs a large, geographically diverse dataset (CONUS, 2000–2024) and incorporates practical metadata (DOY, sensor type, MODIS availability), showing attention to deployment realities.

Rebuttal commits to substantive improvements: correcting equations (e.g., the integral in Section 2.3), detailing MODIS→Landsat alignment and band mapping, specifying baseline configurations, adding strong classical and deep fusion baselines (ESTARFM, HISTARFM, STAIR, FSDAF, CNN/transformer models), extensive ablations (σ, training path, masking probability, composite windows, solver/step trade-offs), real-cloud validation, compute/memory reporting, and code/pipeline release.
weaknesses: As submitted, clarity and reproducibility are insufficient: equations/algorithms have typographical issues (e.g., ambiguous integral in Section 2.3), a figure reference is broken, and key implementation details (MODIS-to-Landsat band mapping, resampling/reprojection choices, STARFM setup) are missing.

Evaluation scope in the manuscript is limited: omits stronger spatiotemporal fusion baselines (ESTARFM/HISTARFM, STAIR variants, FSDAF, recent deep models), lacks ablations on σ, training path, masking probability, composite window, and solver/step trade-offs, provides minimal per-band/spectral analyses (e.g., SAM), and leans heavily on synthetic cloud masks without quantitative validation on real cloudy scenes.

Data processing choices that likely impact results (composite look-back windows and mosaicking rules, MODIS temporal interpolation strategy) are not fully specified or evaluated for sensitivity, leaving generalizability and fairness uncertain.

The flow-matching setup (constant supervision vector field, fixed σ) is not justified in the current text, and stability/sensitivity analyses are absent; convergence behavior and robustness are not demonstrated.
Practical aspects (compute time per tile/step, memory footprint, throughput) and uncertainty characterization are not reported in the current manuscript, limiting assessability for operational use.
reviewers.summary_comment: This work presents a practical and promising framework for generating high-resolution, gap-free reflectance by fusing MODIS and Landsat with Conditional Flow Matching and a mask-aware inpainting strategy. The reported results are encouraging—efficiency improves with moderate step counts (Table 1), and the method outperforms STARFM and a diffusion baseline (Table 2), with clear benefits from MODIS conditioning under heavy cloud cover (Table 3). However, the submitted paper has material clarity and reproducibility issues (broken equations/figure, sparse preprocessing and baseline details), and the evaluation is narrow relative to the state of the art (missing strong classical and deep baselines, ablations, and real-cloud validation). The rebuttal is thorough and addresses these concerns with concrete, comprehensive plans and a commitment to code/pipeline release. If implemented, these revisions would likely elevate the work to acceptance. At present, without the expanded experiments and corrected exposition in the manuscript, the submission does not yet meet the bar for clarity and experimental rigor. I recommend rejection with encouragement to resubmit after integrating the promised additions and analyses.

why_it_fits: Topic fit: Uses conditional flow matching (a modern generative modeling approach) and a U-Net-based architecture to solve a challenging ML problem in remote sensing; aligns with ICML interests in probabilistic methods, deep learning, and application-driven ML (sustainability, environmental monitoring).

Contribution fit: Unifies MODIS-to-Landsat downscaling and cloud/scanline gap filling via a mask-guided inpainting update within a single generative framework; Reviewer A highlights this integration as a clear methodological contribution for application-driven ML.

Empirical rigor (partial): Demonstrates strong quantitative gains over diffusion and STARFM (Table 2: SID 0.0186, SSIM 0.912, PSNR 31.8) and efficient inference with linearized paths (Table 1), which speaks to ML systems/optimization concerns.
why_it_does_not_fit: Rigor fit concerns: Two reviewers (B, C) cite inadequate evaluation breadth in the submitted version (limited baselines, no real-cloud/shadow quantitative tests, sparse configuration details), which is risky for ICML’s high bar on methodological validation.

Presentation/reproducibility gaps: Equation/notation errors (e.g., Eq. (4) ambiguity), a broken figure reference, and missing implementation details (MODIS→Landsat alignment, baseline setups); code/data not yet available. ICML typically expects polished exposition and reproducibility.

Novelty fit risk: Much of the novelty lies in adapting conditional flow matching and integrating a mask-aware update for this application; with two reviewers rating novelty low-to-moderate and several additions promised but not yet integrated, the perceived ML contribution may be viewed as incremental for ICML.

why_it_fits: Topic fit: Strong alignment with NeurIPS tracks such as AI/ML for sustainability, data-centric AI, probabilistic methods, and deep learning; multi-sensor fusion for Earth observation is squarely in scope for interdisciplinary submissions.

Contribution fit: An application-forward generative framework that demonstrates clear empirical benefits and practical impact (CONUS-scale dataset, metadata conditioning, gap-free reflectance generation), which matches NeurIPS’ openness to impactful interdisciplinary work.

Evidence of merit: Outperforms diffusion and STARFM on held-out years (Table 2) and shows efficiency with few inference steps (Table 1); Reviewer A deems the approach sound with notable improvements and strong planned expansions (additional baselines, real-cloud tests, uncertainty diagnostics).
why_it_does_not_fit: Rigor/presentation risks: As submitted, evaluation breadth and fairness are limited (missing established classical and deep spatiotemporal fusion baselines; no quantitative real-cloud validation), and clarity issues persist (equations/figures, missing details, no code), echoing Reviewers B and C.

Novelty calibration: The core ML novelty is an application of conditional flow matching with a mask-aware inpainting strategy; two reviewers rate novelty and soundness as moderate/low, which can be penalized at NeurIPS without the promised expanded experiments.

Operational robustness concerns: Reviewers note sensitivity to QA mask errors and abrupt spectral changes (snow, fires) and currently limited uncertainty calibration—these gaps could undermine acceptance without the pledged robustness analyses.

why_it_fits: Topic fit: Deep learning emphasis with generative modeling (conditional flow matching vs diffusion), U-Net architecture with attention, and training/inference design are within ICLR’s scope.
Empirical signal: Efficient linearized flows that compete favorably with diffusion (Table 2) provide an ML-centric angle that can interest ICLR’s generative modeling community.
Planned analyses (per Reviewer A) such as ablations on σ, probability paths (rectified/VP-like), and stochasticity could strengthen the deep learning contribution if fully integrated.
why_it_does_not_fit: Contribution fit gap: The paper primarily adapts existing flow-matching machinery to a remote sensing application and integrates a mask-aware update; limited new theory/architectural advances may be viewed as insufficient ML novelty for ICLR’s core audience.

Rigor/presentation: Reviewers B and C flag missing baselines, real-cloud validation, and clarity/notation errors; ICLR typically expects clean exposition and thorough ablations at submission time, plus code availability.

Risk from application tilt: The work’s value is largely application/system-level (pipeline for MODIS–Landsat fusion) rather than advancing representation learning per se, making venue fit and acceptance likelihood more borderline compared to NeurIPS’ interdisciplinary tracks.

strengths: Strong, concrete novelty: a $1M real-payout benchmark of 1,488 Upwork-sourced tasks spanning IC SWE and SWE Manager tracks, mapping capability to economic value. On the Diamond split, Claude 3.5 Sonnet reaches 26.2% IC SWE and 44.9% Manager, earning $208k; on the full set it exceeds $400k (Table 1; Figure 6).

Realistic, tamper-resistant grading via triple-verified Playwright end-to-end tests, offline execution, and a private holdout. The zip/postcode example shows E2E tests catching cross-component behavior and global edge cases (e.g., country-specific formats, non-zip countries) that unit tests would miss (A7, A9).

Management dimension is thoughtfully constructed: selection among real proposals with 99% agreement to original manager choices; the rebuttal commits to adding Cohen’s kappa, top-2/MRR, and pairwise NDCG-style preference metrics with rubrics, plus disagreement rationales and contamination-screening scripts.

Insightful ablations: pass@k shows sizable gains (o1 nearly triples with k up to 7; Figure 7); test-time compute improves o1 IC pass@1 from 9.3% to 16.5% and earnings from $16k to $29k (Figure 8); user-tool ablation indicates modest average drop despite 90–120s latency (Figure 9).

Author response improves credibility and future utility: plans to release curation scripts, report rank correlations to external benchmarks, add multimodal and constrained clarification tracks, provide pre-recorded user-tool traces, and conduct economic sensitivity analyses with correlations and normalized payouts.
weaknesses: Single-repository scope (Expensify) still limits technology/domain diversity and backend/infra coverage; while a roadmap and community curation scripts are promised, the current release remains centered on one ecosystem.

Manager-task ground truth, though validated (99% agreement), may not be globally optimal; the committed reliability and alternative metrics (kappa, top-2/MRR, pairwise preferences) are not yet incorporated into the current results.

Economic analysis remains illustrative with simplifying assumptions; payout–difficulty is an imperfect, time-dependent proxy despite forthcoming sensitivity analyses, partial correlations, and inflation normalization.

Interaction constraints (no clarifying questions, text-only despite available videos/screenshots) restrict ecological validity; optional multimodal/clarification/dev-server tracks and pre-recorded traces are planned but not yet realized.
reviewers.summary_comment: SWE-Lancer is a timely, ambitious benchmark that meaningfully advances evaluation of real-world software engineering and management by tying performance to $1M of actual payouts and using triple-verified end-to-end tests. Results are unsaturated and instructive (e.g., 26.2% IC SWE and 44.9% Manager on Diamond for Claude 3.5 Sonnet, $208k earnings), with clear evidence that increased attempts and test-time compute help. The authors’ response materially strengthens soundness and future impact with concrete additions: inter-annotator reliability and alternative metrics for Manager tasks, enhanced contamination checks, sensitivity analyses for the economic modeling, rank correlations with external benchmarks, multimodal and clarification tracks, and open curation scripts. While the single-repo scope and interaction constraints remain present in this version, the core idea is novel, significant, and well-executed. I recommend acceptance.

strengths: Introduces a large, realistic benchmark grounded in 1,488 real Upwork SWE tasks with market-derived payouts ($1M total), and a public Diamond split of 502 tasks worth $500.8k; evaluates both IC SWE and SWE Manager tasks, mapping performance to economic value (Table 1; Figure 6).

Methodological rigor: end-to-end Playwright tests triple-verified by professional engineers; offline, Dockerized execution with no internet, removed remotes, and a private holdout with reintroduced-but-equivalent bugs to mitigate contamination (Sections 2–3; A11).

Comprehensive evaluation and ablations: clear model comparisons and analyses including pass@k gains (Figure 7), test-time compute effects (o1 improves from 9.3% to 16.5% pass@1 and from ~$16k to ~$29k earnings on IC SWE Diamond; Figure 8), and the user-tool ablation showing minimal average drop but larger effects for stronger models (Figure 9).

Empirical headroom is demonstrated: on Diamond, Claude 3.5 Sonnet reaches 26.2% pass@1 on IC SWE and 44.9% on SWE Manager, earning about $208k; on the full set, it earns ~$403k, confirming the benchmark is not saturated (Table 1; Figures 5–6).

The rebuttal offers concrete, rigor-enhancing commitments: multi-seed means with 95% bootstrap CIs and permutation tests, richer SWE Manager metrics (top-2, MRR, pairwise NDCG with agreement statistics and rationales), quantitative test-quality metadata and audits to detect over-constraint/false negatives, economic sensitivity analyses with normalization to constant-year USD, and tools/roadmap to expand beyond a single repository.
weaknesses: External validity remains limited at present: tasks come from one repository with a client-side skew; while the authors plan to add other stacks and infra/DevOps tasks and release a curation pipeline, these extensions are not yet incorporated.

SWE Manager evaluation remains anchored to the original manager’s choice; although 99% agreement is reported and the authors commit to top-2/MRR/NDCG and IAA reporting, current main results emphasize single-choice accuracy.

Statistical rigor in the current version is constrained: many reported numbers are single pass@1 runs without confidence intervals or formal significance tests; the promised multi-seed and bootstrap analyses are planned but not yet included in the main tables.

Economic analysis is explicitly preliminary and based on historical payouts and simplifying assumptions (API pricing, verification latency); sensitivity analyses, normalization, and robustness checks are promised but not yet in the main text.

Quantitative E2E test coverage/robustness metrics and audits of alternate valid fixes are not yet reported; reliance on expert verification is strong, but systematic false-negative/over-constraint rates remain to be quantified.
reviewers.summary_comment: The paper presents SWE-Lancer, a rigorous and timely benchmark that evaluates both individual contributor coding and managerial decision-making on 1,488 real freelance tasks with actual payouts. The evaluation harness is carefully controlled (offline Dockerized environment, contamination safeguards), and grading uses triple-verified end-to-end tests that better reflect full-stack user-visible behavior than unit tests. Results show substantial room for improvement—e.g., on the public Diamond split, Claude 3.5 Sonnet achieves 26.2% pass@1 on IC SWE and 44.9% on SWE Manager, earning roughly $208k (Table 1; Figure 6); test-time compute boosts o1 from 9.3% to 16.5% pass@1 with earnings rising from ~$16k to ~$29k (Figure 8), and pass@k curves show consistent gains (Figure 7). The authors’ rebuttal directly addresses rigor concerns with concrete plans: multi-seed CIs and permutation tests, expanded manager-task metrics and agreement reporting, quantitative test-quality indicators and audits for over-constraint/false negatives, economic sensitivity analyses and payout normalization, and a pipeline and roadmap for multi-repository expansion. While many enhancements are commitments rather than already implemented changes, the current methodology and empirical evidence are solid, and the planned additions meaningfully mitigate remaining concerns. Given its realism, careful design, and clear community value, I recommend acceptance.

strengths: High-practicality benchmark grounded in 1,488 real freelance tasks with $1M in actual payouts; the public Diamond split includes 502 tasks worth $500.8k, mapping performance to economic value.

Robust, triple-verified end-to-end Playwright tests and an offline, Dockerized harness reduce grader hacking and reflect real user flows; the zip/postcode validation case illustrates how E2E captures cross-component failures that unit tests can miss.

Introduces SWE Manager proposal-selection tasks with strong validity (99% agreement with original managerial choices); the authors commit to report inter-annotator agreement (e.g., Cohen’s kappa), top-2/MRR, and pairwise/NDCG-style metrics to further strengthen assessment.

Substantive empirical findings with clear anchoring: on Diamond, Claude 3.5 Sonnet reaches 26.2% on IC SWE and 44.9% on Manager tasks, earning ~$208k (Table 1; Figure 6); pass@k boosts success (Figure 7); higher test-time compute improves o1 IC pass@1 from 9.3% to 16.5% with greater gains on harder tasks (Figure 8); user-tool ablation effects are analyzed (Figure 9); preliminary cost analysis indicates up to ~33.5% savings at pass@5 for o1 (Figure 10).

Strong reproducibility and forward commitments: unified Docker image and open Diamond split today; camera-ready plans include multi-seed means with 95% CIs, stratified bootstraps, permutation tests, test-quality metadata (fixed/broken differentials, flakiness, runtime, coverage proxies), contamination screening (token/AST-level similarity) with per-task cutoff labels, a blinded server for the private holdout, and curation scripts plus a roadmap to expand to additional stacks (backend-heavy, mobile-native) and infra/DevOps tasks; optional multimodal and clarification tracks are planned.
weaknesses: Scope remains single-repository and JS/TS-heavy with underrepresentation of backend and infrastructure/DevOps tasks; the planned expansion is promising but not yet realized, so cross-domain generalization is still uncertain.

Manager-task ground truth remains tied to historical choices; even with planned top-2/MRR and NDCG-style metrics and agreement reporting, residual subjectivity and non-uniqueness of an optimal proposal can persist.

Partial openness persists: a private holdout limits full community auditing; although a blinded server and time-delayed sanitized release are planned, contamination risks inherent to public issues remain despite offline execution and screening scripts.

Headline results currently rely on single pass@1 runs for some models; while the authors commit to multi-seed CIs and significance tests in the camera-ready, the present statistical reporting is limited; multimodal and constrained-clarification tracks are forward-looking and not yet reflected in reported numbers.
reviewers.summary_comment: The paper delivers a rigorous, economically grounded benchmark of real freelance software engineering work with end-to-end grading and a novel management-evaluation component. Results are well contextualized: e.g., on the Diamond split Claude 3.5 Sonnet earns ~$208k with 26.2% IC and 44.9% Manager pass@1 (Table 1; Figure 6), pass@k boosts success (Figure 7), increased test-time compute helps on harder tasks (Figure 8), user-tool effects are analyzed (Figure 9), and a preliminary price analysis suggests up to ~33.5% savings at pass@5 for o1 (Figure 10). The rebuttal substantially strengthens prior concerns by committing to: multi-seed confidence intervals and formal significance tests; richer manager-task metrics (top-2/MRR, NDCG, kappa) and rationales; contamination screening (token/AST similarity) with per-task cutoff labels; test-quality metadata; a blinded server and delayed sanitized holdout release; and a roadmap with tooling to add distinct-stack repositories and an infra/DevOps slice, plus optional multimodal and clarification tracks. While some improvements are forward-looking and the single-repo scope and ground-truth subjectivity cannot be fully eliminated, the benchmark’s practical value, methodological rigor, and clarity are compelling. I recommend acceptance and encourage the camera-ready to include the promised multi-seed CIs and significance tests for headline metrics, the expanded manager-task reporting, and concrete progress on the second-repository expansion.

why_it_fits: Topic fit: ICML explicitly welcomes evaluation methodology, meta studies, replicability/validity, and application-driven ML; this work is a large-scale evaluation benchmark for LLM-based software engineering with rigorous grading and economic grounding, aligning with ICML’s evaluation and systems interests.

Contribution fit: The primary contribution is a benchmark plus an evaluation harness (triple-verified end-to-end Playwright tests, offline Dockerized setup, private holdout) and systematic ablations (pass@k, test-time compute, tool use). Reviewers repeatedly praised the methodological rigor and clear ablations (e.g., o1 pass@1 rising from 9.3% to 16.5% with higher reasoning effort; pass@k gains).

Novelty fit: Reviewers highlighted concrete novelty—mapping performance to $1M in actual payouts and introducing SWE Manager selection tasks with 99% agreement to historical choices—indicating a level of novelty appropriate for a top venue.

Rigor fit: Strong engineering controls (no internet, removed remotes, private holdout with reintroduced bugs) and triple-verified E2E tests reduce grader hacking, which reviewers emphasized as a strength; the results are unsaturated with clear headroom (e.g., 26.2% pass@1 IC SWE, 44.9% Manager on Diamond).

Presentation fit: Clarity and reproducibility were consistently praised by reviewers (open-sourced Diamond split, unified Docker image, clear reporting), with camera-ready commitments to add multi-seed CIs, permutation tests, and richer Manager metrics (top-2/MRR/NDCG, kappa).
Reviewer risk: All three reviewers recommend acceptance and view the work as timely and impactful, reducing risk for an ICML submission that values robust evaluation work.
why_it_does_not_fit: Topic emphasis mismatch: ICML often prioritizes new algorithms or theory; this paper is primarily a benchmark and evaluation methodology rather than a new learning method, which could be viewed as secondary in some ICML tracks.

Scope limitation flagged by reviewers: Single-repository focus (Expensify) with JS/TS skew and underrepresentation of backend/infra tasks may be seen as too narrow for ICML’s broad ML audience until the promised multi-repo expansion lands.

Rigor reservations (current version): Reviewers noted many headline numbers are single pass@1 runs without confidence intervals and that some robustness analyses (e.g., economic sensitivity, normalized payouts) are promised but not yet fully incorporated; ICML may expect these to be present at submission time.

Ecological validity gaps: No clarifying questions, text-only inputs despite available videos/screenshots; reviewers view future multimodal/clarification tracks positively, but they are not yet realized in the reported results.

Potentially stronger home elsewhere: NeurIPS has a well-established Datasets & Benchmarks track and explicit data-centric/SysML emphasis, which may make that venue a more natural fit than ICML for a benchmark-centric paper.

why_it_fits: Topic fit: NeurIPS has a Datasets & Benchmarks track and highlights data-centric AI, SysML infrastructure, socio-technical aspects, and AI-in-practice; this benchmark squarely targets LLM-for-code agents with realistic E2E grading and economic mapping—highly aligned with NeurIPS focus areas.

Contribution fit: A large, realistic benchmark (1,488 Upwork-derived tasks; $1M payouts) plus a controlled evaluation harness and strong ablations fits the NeurIPS D&B/systems ethos. Reviewers called out the novel SWE Manager task, the triple-verified E2E tests, and the offline Dockerized setup as major strengths.

Novelty fit: Reviewers scored novelty well and emphasized new dimensions (market-derived payouts, managerial decision-making with 99% agreement) and unsaturated results (e.g., 26.2% IC SWE, 44.9% Manager on Diamond), matching NeurIPS expectations for impactful benchmarks.

Rigor fit: The paper demonstrates careful controls (no internet, removed remotes, holdout with reintroduced bugs) and systematic analyses (pass@k, test-time compute, user-tool ablations, preliminary cost analysis). Reviewers also noted camera-ready commitments to multi-seed CIs, permutation tests, expanded Manager metrics, and contamination screening—all valued in NeurIPS D&B.

Presentation fit: Reviewers praised clarity, open-sourced Diamond split and Docker image, and concrete plans to expand reporting and tracks (multimodal, constrained clarification). NeurIPS also values broader impacts; the paper includes an Impact Statement and economic framing.
Reviewer risk: All reviewers recommend acceptance with strong endorsements of significance and community utility, suggesting low risk of negative reception in NeurIPS’s benchmarking ecosystem.
why_it_does_not_fit: Scope concerns: Reviewers noted single-repository scope and client-side skew; while NeurIPS D&B often accepts focused benchmarks, broader domain diversity (multiple stacks/infra) would further strengthen generality.

Evaluation details pending: Some statistical rigor (multi-seed CIs, permutation tests) and richer Manager-task metrics (top-2/MRR/NDCG, kappa) are promised for the camera-ready but are not yet reflected in the main tables.

Ground-truth subjectivity: Manager-task evaluation relies on historical choices (despite 99% validation); NeurIPS D&B may ask for more extensive preference/IA agreement reporting in the main paper, not just in planned additions.
Economic analysis limits: Reviewers regarded the cost-savings analysis as illustrative with simplifying assumptions; additional sensitivity/normalization analyses are planned but not yet included.

why_it_fits: Topic fit: ICLR welcomes datasets and benchmarks, deep learning applications, and ML systems evaluations; this work evaluates frontier LLMs (deep learning models) on realistic software-engineering tasks with E2E tests—squarely relevant to the DL community.

Contribution fit: The benchmark, offline harness, and ablations (pass@k curves, test-time compute effects, tool-use analysis) constitute substantive empirical contributions, which reviewers praised for rigor and clarity.

Novelty fit: Reviewers highlighted novel aspects—economic mapping to $1M payouts and the SWE Manager task with high agreement—providing new angles on LLM agent capabilities beyond standard coding benchmarks.

Rigor fit: Triple-verified Playwright E2E tests, private holdout with reintroduced bugs, and contamination safeguards align with ICLR’s emphasis on reliable evaluation; commitments to multi-seed CIs and permutation tests further improve rigor.
Presentation fit: Clear writing and reproducibility (open Diamond split and Docker image) were emphasized by reviewers, aligning with ICLR’s expectations for well-documented benchmarks.
why_it_does_not_fit: Venue emphasis: ICLR often prioritizes learning-method advances or insights into representation/optimization; this work is benchmark-centric rather than proposing new DL methods or theory, which could face higher bar outside the Datasets & Benchmarks track.
Scope limitations: Reviewers noted single-repository coverage with underrepresentation of backend/infra tasks; ICLR audiences may question generalization until multi-repo expansion materializes.

Statistical reporting and metrics: Some results are single pass@1 runs without CIs; expanded Manager-task metrics and test-quality audits are promised but not fully present, which may be scrutinized by ICLR reviewers focused on rigor in quantitative reporting.

Ecological realism gaps: No multimodality or clarifying questions in the reported setup; while future tracks are planned, current results don’t yet reflect these dimensions, which may limit perceived completeness.

strengths: Compellingly novel framing of cross-task, multi-turn in-context RL via self-generated trajectory preferences (multi-turn DPO/RPO on action tokens), with zero-shot transfer across 10 task groups and two base models; notable gains include bandit best-arm success improving from 42.25% to 62.25% in LOO and an average improvement of ~47% over the original success rate after ~22,500 trajectories.

The rebuttal substantially strengthens methodological rigor: majority-vote judging (two LLM judges plus rule-consistency), re-scoring with a stronger judge (GPT-4o), separate reporting for programmatic vs. LLM-simulated groups, increased evaluation rollouts per test task (from 4 to 16) with 95% CIs, and public release of anonymized transcripts for auditing.

Concrete corrections and ablations: fixing Minesweeper preference mismatch (SFT-only or KTO with first-action matching), ablations on negative selection (random vs worst vs mined hard negatives) with token-level margin statistics, stabilized ν (CV) via ε floors, winsorization, and empirical-Bayes shrinkage, and sensitivity analyses to grouping noise for curriculum.

Broader and stronger baselines promised: STaR-style and RAFT-style variants under identical data budgets, plus a small-scale RM+PPO online RL baseline on programmatic groups, with compute and data cost reporting for fair comparisons.

Practicality and transparency: plans for equal-performance-at-lower-sample-cost curves for curriculum vs uniform sampling, temperature/Min-p and data-subsampling ablations, budget-normalized results, a cost-reduction toolkit (early filtering, guided exploration prompts, hard-negative reuse), and scripts for curriculum and filtering.
weaknesses: Several improvements are promised for the camera-ready but are not yet reflected in the current results (e.g., revised judging/auditing, 16-rollout evaluations with CIs, Minesweeper preference fix, STaR/RAFT and small-scale online RL baselines). Please clarify scope and coverage: will majority-vote judging and 16-rollout evaluations apply across all groups or only LLM-simulated ones, and what is the size of the GPT-4o re-scoring sample?

Residual dependence on LLM-simulated environments implies nonzero bias despite added auditing; curriculum gains to date are modest and generality across groups remains to be demonstrated empirically. Negative transfer (e.g., Wordle) persists; please include the promised diagnostics (e.g., per-trajectory length distributions, action-token margin stats, formatting adherence) and ablations (negative mining, multi-objective variant, CoT prompting) with per-group CIs and variance across seeds.

Adoption may be limited by trajectory generation cost and reliance on self-generated data quality and base model strength. A concise, compute-normalized cost breakdown (API cost, GPU hours, trajectories/hour, wall-clock profile of curriculum vs uniform) would help practitioners assess feasibility.
reviewers.summary_comment: The authors’ second response credibly addresses the remaining concerns with concrete, feasible commitments: redundant judging with stronger re-scoring, separate reporting for programmatic vs LLM environments, increased evaluation rollouts and CIs, a principled fix for the Minesweeper pairing issue, broader curriculum validation with efficiency curves and robustness analyses, stronger baselines (STaR/RAFT and small-scale RM+PPO), stabilized ν estimation, and practical cost controls. These changes should further bolster the soundness and reliability of the reported gains, which already include cross-task generalization (e.g., bandits 42.25%→62.25%), consistent improvements across 10 task groups, and reduced turns to solve tasks. Some risk remains because many upgrades are promised rather than already included, and LLM-environment bias cannot be fully eliminated. However, the core idea is clearly novel and impactful, the empirical evidence is strong and improving, and the planned additions address key methodological concerns. I maintain an Accept recommendation contingent on integrating the promised analyses and reporting details.

strengths: The authors commit to concrete validity upgrades: majority-vote judging (two independent judges plus rule-consistency), re-scoring with a stronger judge, separate reporting for programmatic vs LLM-simulated tasks, stratified bootstrap confidence intervals, and public release of anonymized transcripts for external auditing.

Baseline and analysis breadth will be substantially expanded: STaR-style self-training, RAFT-style reward-ranked fine-tuning, a small-scale online RL baseline on programmatic tasks with compute/data reporting, and guided-exploration data generation ablations, all under matched budgets.

Curriculum rigor is strengthened with stabilized coefficient-of-variation estimation (ε floor, winsorization/median-IQR, shrinkage), normalized proxy rewards, accuracy–budget curves with AUC comparisons, and sensitivity to grouping noise; evaluation rollouts increase (4→16) with token-level margin statistics and ablations on negative selection policies.

Technical mismatches are addressed with actionable fixes: Minesweeper preference-pairing corrected via SFT-only, KTO-style objectives, or filtered pairs with identical first actions, with side-by-side deltas reported.

Greater emphasis on programmatic or externally validated evaluations: foregrounding aggregate results on six programmatic groups, promoting modified programmatic Wordle in the main text, moving LMRL-Gym evaluations to the main body, and releasing per-episode logs and curriculum traces to facilitate independent re-scoring.
weaknesses: Most improvements are promised for the camera-ready and not yet reflected in the current figures; acceptance is contingent on delivering the expanded baselines, equal-performance-at-lower-sample-cost curriculum curves, and enhanced judging/auditing as specified.

Residual dependence on LLM-simulated environments may still introduce subtle biases even with improved judging; programmatic-only and externally validated results should be emphasized in the main paper with adjusted success rates reported after re-scoring.

Curriculum gains have been modest so far; the planned stabilization and AUC-based efficiency demonstrations need to clearly show equal-performance at lower sampling budgets and include sensitivity analyses, with a fully reproducible ν computation pipeline.
reviewers.summary_comment: The follow-up response directly addresses prior concerns with a detailed and feasible plan that strengthens evaluation validity, comparison breadth, and methodological rigor. The promised additions—redundant judging with stronger models, separate reporting for programmatic vs simulated environments, transcript releases, expanded baselines (STaR/RAFT, guided exploration, and a small-scale online RL comparison), stabilized curriculum metrics with budget–accuracy curves, and fixes to Minesweeper pairing—would materially improve the soundness and interpretability of the results without altering the core contribution. While these enhancements are not yet reflected in the present figures, the existing evidence already shows broad performance gains, cross-group generalization, and efficiency improvements without degradation on standard benchmarks. I maintain an acceptance recommendation and strongly encourage incorporating the promised analyses in the camera-ready to fully substantiate the claims.

strengths: Practical and reproducible framework for teaching general multi-turn, partially observable decision-making via self-generated trajectories and preference tuning; code, datasets, and a model checkpoint are released.

Diverse 10-group task suite with six programmatic, deterministic environments; the rebuttal commits to separating programmatic vs LLM-simulated results, reporting confidence intervals, and releasing anonymized transcripts for external auditing.

Simple, scalable pipeline (high-temperature Min-p sampling, SFT + multi-turn DPO/RPO on action tokens) with a decoupled data-collection workflow; authors justify action-token-only objectives and propose stronger auditing (majority-vote judges, stronger re-scoring, leakage checks, adversarial probes).

Compelling evidence of cross-task generalization and improved efficiency (fewer turns) on two base models without regression on standard benchmarks; authors will increase evaluation rollouts (to 16) and add bootstrapped confidence intervals for robustness.

Concrete camera-ready upgrades: broader baselines (STaR-style self-refinement, RAFT, guided exploration like BAGEL, and small-scale online RL), curriculum efficiency curves across additional groups with ν-regularization and batched UCB, and a technical fix for Minesweeper preference pairing (SFT-only/KTO or filtered pairs).

Additional practical guidance promised: cost controls for data collection (curriculum-driven sampling, early stopping, cascaded judging), budget-normalized curves, per-trajectory token budgets, and engineering scripts for curriculum selection and logging.
weaknesses: Several key improvements (majority-vote judging, stronger re-scoring, transcript release, increased rollouts with CIs, expanded baselines) are planned for the camera-ready and not yet reflected in the current results; residual bias from LLM-simulated environments remains a risk until these audits are executed.

Curriculum gains are modest and currently demonstrated on a single group; effectiveness depends on metadata quality and small-sample ν estimates, though the planned cross-group validation and regularization should help.

Comparative evaluation breadth is still limited in the present submission (e.g., guided exploration, online RL, and self-refinement baselines are pending), so some claims about efficiency and generality remain constrained until those results are added.
reviewers.summary_comment: This work proposes PAPRIKA, a clear and scalable preference-tuning framework that equips LLMs with general multi-turn exploration and decision-making capabilities using self-generated data across a diverse suite of tasks. The approach is well-motivated, practically oriented, and demonstrates cross-task generalization and improved task efficiency without degrading standard benchmarks. The authors’ rebuttal substantively addresses practical concerns by committing to stronger auditing of LLM-simulated environments (majority-vote judges, stronger re-scoring, transcript release, and separate reporting), expanding baselines (STaR/RAFT, guided exploration, and small-scale online RL), increasing evaluation rollouts with confidence intervals, and broadening and regularizing the curriculum analysis with efficiency curves and batched UCB. While many of these enhancements are prospective for the camera-ready version, the core contribution is already valuable, and the planned additions will further strengthen reliability and practical relevance. I recommend acceptance.

why_it_fits: Strong topic alignment with reinforcement learning, decision-making, optimization, and evaluation: PAPRIKA fine-tunes LLMs for multi-turn, partially observable sequential decision-making using preference optimization and a UCB-style curriculum, all squarely within ICML’s core areas.

Methodological contribution beyond application: a multi-turn, action-token DPO/RPO setup, a data-centric pipeline for self-generated trajectories, and a principled curriculum objective (coefficient of variation with UCB) that targets learning potential across task groups.

Emphasis on rigor and reproducibility matches ICML norms: code/dataset/model release; reviewers note added validity upgrades (majority-vote judging, stronger re-scoring, increased rollouts with 95% CIs, separate reporting for programmatic vs LLM envs).

Breadth of empirical evaluation across 10 task groups (6 programmatic) and two base models, with cross-task generalization and task-efficiency gains (fewer turns), aligns with ICML’s expectation for solid, well-controlled experiments.

Clear positioning within meta-RL/in-context RL literature, with curriculum ablations and negative-sampling analyses planned, speaks to the community’s interest in scalable learning systems and evaluation methodology.
why_it_does_not_fit: Current paper state relies partly on LLM-simulated environments; reviewers highlight residual bias despite planned majority-vote judging and re-scoring. ICML often expects high bar for evaluation validity, which may hinge on delivering promised audits.

Several critical analyses are promised for camera-ready (16-rollout CIs, STaR/RAFT and small RM+PPO baselines, Minesweeper pairing fix, cost breakdowns) but not yet fully reflected; ICML tends to penalize reliance on prospective additions.
Curriculum gains are modest so far and validated primarily on a single group; ICML audiences may expect stronger cross-group efficiency evidence or theory to support the selection rule.
Negative transfer on some groups (e.g., Wordle) and remaining dependence on base-model quality/data-generation quality may temper perceived generality.
Adoption cost and compute/sample budget concerns need the promised compute-normalized reporting; without it, ICML reviewers may question practical feasibility at scale.

why_it_fits: Excellent topical resonance with NeurIPS tracks on decision-making/RL/control, data-centric AI, deep learning, SysML infrastructure, and human/AI interaction: a broadly relevant agent-training pipeline for LLMs with multi-turn reasoning and exploration.

Strong empirical systems flavor and interdisciplinary appeal: self-generated data, preference optimization, curriculum learning, and cross-task transfer across diverse textual and programmatic environments match NeurIPS’s appetite for impactful empirical ML systems.

Reviewers report solid current evidence and credible camera-ready upgrades (majority-vote judging, stronger re-scoring, 16-rollout CIs, expanded baselines including STaR/RAFT and a small online RL baseline), which aligns with NeurIPS’s emphasis on comprehensive empirical studies.
Clear, practical framing and released artifacts (code, datasets, model checkpoint) support NeurIPS’s reproducibility and community uptake goals.
NeurIPS explicitly welcomes in-depth analyses of existing methods and data-centric pipelines; the paper’s ablations on negative selection, curriculum stabilization, and cost controls fit that ethos.
why_it_does_not_fit: Some core validity and baseline additions are planned rather than already incorporated; acceptance likelihood remains strong per reviewers, but residual risk persists until audits and broader baselines are fully delivered.

Continued reliance on LLM-simulated environments—even with improved judging—can leave subtle biases; the paper must foreground programmatic-only results and adjusted scores after re-scoring to mitigate concerns.
Curriculum benefits, though present, are currently modest; demonstrating equal-performance-at-lower-sample-cost across multiple groups will be important for NeurIPS’s empirical standards.
Negative transfer cases (e.g., Wordle) need diagnostic analyses and mitigation strategies (e.g., task weighting, multi-objective tuning) to strengthen generality claims.

why_it_fits: Directly relevant to ICLR’s focus on deep learning, representation/optimization, and RL: teaches LLMs in-context RL behaviors via preference optimization and a curriculum over tasks.
Method centers on post-training/fine-tuning objectives (multi-turn DPO/RPO on action tokens) and data-centric design choices, which are common at ICLR for agentic and alignment-style works.
Empirical generalization across unseen task groups and reduced-turn efficiency provide meaningful evidence that the learned representations support in-context decision-making.
Commitments to stronger baselines, increased rollouts with CIs, and public artifacts align with ICLR’s reproducibility and analysis expectations.
why_it_does_not_fit: ICLR audiences often seek clearer conceptual or theoretical insights into representation learning or learning dynamics; here, novelty is primarily a practical pipeline (multi-turn DPO/RPO + CV/UCB curriculum) with limited new theory.
Reliance on LLM-simulated environments and several promised but not-yet-shown upgrades may undercut perceived rigor compared to ICLR’s bar for careful, principled analyses.
Curriculum efficiency gains are still modest and shown on a limited scope; stronger cross-group validation or theory for the CV-based selection would improve fit.

Novelty perception is mixed per reviewers (novelty scores around moderate), which at ICLR can reduce acceptance odds unless the final version adds deeper insight (e.g., analysis of why in-context RL emerges, ablation on representation changes).
Negative transfer and dependence on base model/data quality remain, suggesting more work is needed to articulate principled failure modes and mitigations.

strengths: The authors further temper and precisely calibrate their claims, clearly separating partner vs. environment diversity and explicitly framing hybridization (CEC + partner diversity) as promising but unvalidated within the current budget.

Improved transparency and practicality: they will add a compute accounting subsection with per-policy steps, parameter counts, wall-clock throughput, and explicit population overheads for baselines, plus practical guidance on where to invest a fixed training budget.

Human-study analysis is strengthened with concrete adaptation metrics (adaptation latency/index, conditional mutual information responsiveness, role-switch rate), multiple-comparison corrections, and effect sizes with confidence intervals, improving the evidence that CEC learns general cooperative norms.

They outline actionable design guidance for future hybrid approaches (larger recurrent cores, longer horizons, staged curricula, partner-entropy schedules), demonstrating thoughtful consideration of variance and convergence challenges.

Clarity is significantly improved by explicitly documenting held-out layouts for CEC, per-policy step parity across methods, and the evaluation axes (cross-partner on seen layouts vs. cross-partner + cross-environment on procedural layouts).
weaknesses: Hybridization remains unvalidated within this work; while a design plan is provided, there is no empirical demonstration of a successful combined-diversity regime under scaled models or longer training.

Human evaluations still span only two layouts and relatively short sessions; despite added metrics and statistical rigor, broader task coverage, longer interactions, and more diverse participant pools are needed to strengthen general human-compatibility claims.

Compute reporting could go further by including explicit GPU-hour totals and throughput across methods and by detailing population sizes/snapshot counts for PBT-style baselines to quantify total cost beyond per-policy parity.
reviewers.summary_comment: The follow-up response meaningfully strengthens the paper by tempering claims, clarifying evaluation axes, and substantially improving transparency and analysis. Added compute accounting and practical guidance address fairness and applicability, while new adaptation metrics, effect-size reporting, and multiple-comparison controls bolster the human-study evidence that CEC learns general cooperative norms. Although hybridization remains future work and human evaluations are still constrained in scope, the core contribution—demonstrating that environment diversity via self-play can be highly effective for zero-shot coordination across partners and tasks—remains novel, significant, and now better substantiated. I maintain my recommendation to accept.

strengths: The authors provide concrete, reproducibility-focused commitments: full baseline configurations (population snapshot schedules, PPO/optimizer settings, seeds), compute accounting (per-policy steps, parameter counts, wall-clock throughput), solvability checks with rejection rates, and release of code and exact evaluation seeds.

Human-study rigor is substantially strengthened with planned Benjamini–Hochberg FDR corrections, effect sizes and confidence intervals, a composite cooperation index, and the addition of mixed-effects models with random effects for participants and seeds—plus objective adaptation metrics (latency, responsiveness, role-switch rates) computed from existing logs.

Diagnostic analyses to explain single-task failures will be added without retraining, including reward histograms on the 100 held-out layouts, sensitivity to rotations/translations and small object perturbations, and rollout visualizations contrasting fixed-route vs. object-centric behavior, improving the fairness and interpretability of comparisons.

The core empirical evidence remains compelling: across AI-AI and human-AI evaluations, training via environment diversity (CEC) yields superior zero-shot coordination to novel partners and tasks under matched training steps, supported by cross-algorithm empirical game-theoretic analysis.
Significant engineering contribution (fast JAX Overcooked generator and training pipeline) with explicit compute and solvability reporting, enhancing methodological soundness and community impact.
weaknesses: Several key improvements are camera-ready commitments rather than currently presented results; baseline hyperparameter sweeps remain limited, so concerns about potential under-optimization of PBT/E3T are reduced but not fully resolved.

Human evaluation scope is still narrow (two layouts, 200-step episodes, English-proficient participants). While mixed-effects modeling and statistical corrections strengthen inference, broader tasks and participant diversity would better substantiate general claims.

The interaction between partner and environment diversity remains inconclusive; despite tempered claims and proposed design adjustments, hybridization is not empirically explored with larger models or extended training. Additional baselines (e.g., light environment augmentation for ST or small partner populations within CEC) are acknowledged but deferred.
reviewers.summary_comment: The authors address prior concerns with substantial, concrete additions: detailed baseline and compute reporting, code/seed release, solvability checks, and several diagnostic and statistical analyses that strengthen fairness and interpretability (FDR-corrected tests, mixed-effects models, objective adaptation metrics, and sensitivity controls). These revisions, together with the existing breadth of AI-AI and human-AI evidence and the engineering contribution, support the central claim that cross-environment self-play induces general cooperative norms transferable across partners and tasks. Some reservations remain—particularly the limited hyperparameter sweeps for PBT/E3T, the narrow human-study scope, and the inconclusive hybridization results—but the methodological soundness and evaluation are sufficiently convincing. I recommend acceptance contingent on inclusion of the promised analyses and reporting.

strengths: The authors calibrated claims around novelty and clearly positioned the contribution as a conceptual and empirical shift toward environment diversity, backed by scalable infrastructure and extensive evaluations.

They commit to thorough reproducibility and fairness: compute accounting tables (steps, parameter counts, wall-clock), detailed baseline configurations (PBT/E3T settings), and release of test seeds and generator code, plus added analyses (reward histograms, micro-perturbation controls, rollout visualizations).

Concrete practical guidance is provided via an actionable training recipe (CEC pretraining for general norms, light fine-tuning for task-optimal reward, optional partner diversity when deployment is fixed), directly useful for practitioners.

Evaluation rigor is strengthened with planned statistical corrections for human studies, effect sizes, and new objective adaptation metrics (adaptation latency, conditional mutual information, role-switch rates), alongside a Pareto analysis of reward vs. collision/adaptivity.
